title: Vector Databases and Similarity Search
concept: Callback to Unit 171/294: vector databases index sentence embeddings, performing fast cosine similarity search to retrieve relevant text chunks.

exercises.prompt: How do computers compare the meanings of two different sentences?

options.text: By converting them into lists of numbers called embeddings
By translating them into Morse code
By storing them as audio files

exercises.explain: Embeddings represent the underlying meaning of words or sentences as numbers, allowing computers to understand similarity.

cards.title: Beyond Keywords
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how ChatGPT remembers facts or finds the right document to answer your questions? It doesn't search like Google does using keywords. Instead, it uses a superpower called 'vector similarity search' to find matching ideas instantly.

cards.title: What is a Vector Database?
cards.body: A vector database is a specialized storage system designed to hold and quickly search through list-of-number representations of data (vectors). By indexing these embeddings, it can search through millions of items in milliseconds to find the ones closest in meaning.

cards.title: The Magical Library
cards.body: Imagine your library has millions of books, but instead of sorting them by author name, they are organized by how they feel. Books about space travel are placed on one high shelf, while books about alien cooking are placed right next to them because they share similar themes. A vector database is like this magical library, grouping similar concepts close together in space.

cards.title: Finding Ideas, Not Just Words
cards.body: If you search 'How do I fix a flat tire?', a traditional database looks for the exact word 'tire'. A vector database searches for the concept, retrieving helpful chunks like 'changing a damaged wheel' even if the word 'flat' or 'tire' isn't in that exact sentence!

cards.title: Myth: Only for Text
cards.body: People often think vector databases only search for text. In reality, because anything can be turned into numbers, you can store and search images, audio tracks, and even user behaviors in a vector database!

cards.title: Keyword vs. Vector Search
cards.body: Let's look at how traditional keyword search compares to vector similarity search when looking for a recipe.

left.text: Looks for exact letters. Misses 'flapjack' if you search 'pancake'.

right.text: Looks for concept patterns. Finds 'flapjack' because it understands it is like a 'pancake'.

exercises.prompt: What do vector databases primarily store and search through?

options.text: Raw text files and HTML code pages
Python script files and database logs

exercises.explain: Vector databases store sentence embeddings (numerical vectors representing meaning) to quickly find similar concepts.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Traditional keyword databases are better at finding similar concepts than vector databases.
exercises.explain: Traditional databases rely on exact keyword matches. Vector databases search by semantic similarity (meaning).

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to identify the common math method used to find similar vector concepts.
exercises.sentence: We often use cosine similarity to measure how close two vectors are in space.
exercises.explain: Cosine similarity measures the angle between vectors to see how close their directions (and meanings) are.

exercises.prompt: Which of these queries represents a vector similarity search?

options.text: SELECT * FROM articles WHERE content LIKE '%space%';

exercises.explain: A standard SQL query searches for exact keyword matches, whereas vector databases search for conceptual similarity using embeddings.

exercises.prompt: How does a vector database find the most relevant chunk of text for a query?

options.text: By looking for exact words like 'recipe' or 'dinner' in a dictionary index
By calculating which text vector is mathematically closest to the query vector
By sorting all stored texts alphabetically and picking the top results
By translating the query into a programming language like Python

exercises.explain: A vector database does not search for exact keywords; it calculates mathematical distances (like cosine similarity) between vectors to find chunks of text with similar meanings.
